subProcess: Local Management System Development and Control
controlName: LMS Document Owner Assignment and Accountability
controlStatement: Who: Site Process Owner (SPO) or equivalent | What: Assigns document owners and establishes accountability for content ownership of LMS sections and controlled documents | When: Upon LMS creation and annually reviewed as part of management system health assessment | Where: Local Management System documentation hierarchy and org structure records | Why: Ensures clear ownership prevents content drift, inconsistency, and accountability gaps; maintains LMS as living system aligned with actual work execution | How: SPO designates responsible person/role per LMS section with written sign-off; documents in org responsibility matrix; tracks via LMS custodian dashboard | Evidence: Responsibility matrix with dated assignments, SPO approval records, LMS health dashboard showing owner assignments current
keyActivities: Identify all LMS sections and controlled documents requiring owners
Assign document owners close to work execution (not centralized custody)
Document assignment in responsibility matrix with roles and titles
Establish review cycle expectations and update frequency per owner
Verify owner competence and authority to approve changes
Track ownership changes and transitions during reorganization
Report LMS ownership health via quarterly management review
riskAddressed: Lack of clear ownership leads to outdated procedures, conflicting guidance, and gap between LMS documentation and actual work practice; creates audit findings and regulatory compliance risk
testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) all major LMS sections have named owner with written assignment; (2) owner role aligns with actual business function; (3) owner approval records exist for recent document updates; (4) ownership changes are formally approved per Management of Change; (5) LMS dashboard/records show current vs. overdue ownership assignments

controlName: Systematic LMS Review and Update Cycle
controlStatement: Who: LMS Custodian/Focal Point and SPOs | What: Conduct systematic review of LMS content on defined cycle; identify gaps and updates needed vs. business objectives and regulatory changes | When: Minimum 2-3 year review cycle for all content; priority areas (SEAM Standards, critical safety procedures) reviewed annually | Where: LMS repository system; review meeting minutes; update prioritization log | Why: Prevents obsolescence and inconsistency between documented processes and actual work; ensures Group and regulatory requirements operationalized; demonstrates management commitment to continuous improvement | How: (1) Annual stakeholder meeting to identify changes in business context, regulations, SEAM Standards, incident learnings; (2) Risk-prioritize update scope; (3) Create update plan with owner assignments and timeline; (4) Track completion via management review dashboard; (5) Communicate changes to affected staff | Evidence: Annual review meeting minutes with attendees, documented change drivers, prioritized update list, completion status reports, sign-off by Asset Manager
keyActivities: Schedule annual LMS health and relevance review meeting with SPOs and Asset Leader
Document changes in business context, regulations, and SEAM Standards implementation
Review prior audit findings and assurance recommendations for LMS gaps
Identify sections requiring update based on risk and business priority
Create update plan with owner, scope, timeline, and resource allocation
Assign accountability for each update to relevant SPO or process owner
Track completion of updates and document in controlled repository
Communicate implemented changes to organization with training where needed
Report LMS update status and completion % in quarterly management review
riskAddressed: LMS becomes stale and disconnected from actual work, regulatory requirements drift unchecked, SEAM Standards implementation incomplete, audit findings accumulate, management system loses effectiveness and credibility
testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) documented annual LMS review meeting with agenda and participants; (2) documented change drivers and regulatory updates addressed in review scope; (3) risk-prioritized list of sections requiring update; (4) LMS update plan with owner assignments and completion timeline; (5) evidence of completed updates (date, owner, approval); (6) sample recent updates checked for accuracy and communication to staff; (7) management review minutes showing LMS status reported

controlName: MoC Process Owner Appointment and Accountability
controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager (or delegate) | What: Appoints MoC Process Owner (typically Technology/Engineering Manager) with explicit accountability for MoC effectiveness; documents appointment and authority | When: Upon asset commissioning or organizational change; formal appointment documented in org structure | Where: Org structure documents, MoC procedure, Asset Manager signed appointment memo | Why: Establishes clear accountability for ensuring MoC process functions per SEAM Standards, prevents uncontrolled changes, ensures hazard assessment and risk mitigation before implementation | How: Asset Manager issues appointment memo specifying: (1) MoC Process Owner role and responsibilities; (2) authority to approve MoC procedures and policy; (3) accountability for assurance and effectiveness review; (4) reporting line and escalation path; (5) competence requirements | Evidence: Signed appointment memo with date, Asset Manager signature, job description reference, documented delegation of authority, MoC procedure signed by appointed owner
keyActivities: Identify candidate for MoC Process Owner role (typically Engineering/Technology lead with credibility)
Define MoC Process Owner responsibilities and authority in writing
Issue formal appointment memo signed by Asset Manager
Ensure appointed owner understands SEAM Standards MoC requirements and best practices
Verify owner competence in process hazard analysis and risk assessment
Establish reporting cadence and escalation path to Asset Manager
Document appointment in org chart and communicate to asset staff
Conduct annual review of MoC PO performance and effectiveness
Plan succession and backup for MoC Process Owner role
riskAddressed: Without clear accountability, MoC process lacks leadership and becomes ineffective; changes bypass proper hazard assessment; risks are not identified/mitigated; incidents result from poorly managed modifications
testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) current org chart shows named MoC Process Owner with reporting line to Asset Manager; (2) signed appointment memo exists with date and Asset Manager approval; (3) job description/procedure shows MoC PO responsibilities match SEAM Standards requirements; (4) MoC PO can articulate key responsibilities and process effectiveness; (5) evidence of MoC PO leading process effectiveness reviews, assurance, and improvement; (6) MoC procedures are formally approved by appointed PO with signature and date

controlName: Management of Change Procedure Compliance Assurance
controlStatement: Who: MoC Process Owner and MoC Process Focal Point | What: Conduct periodic quality assurance of initiated MoCs to verify adherence to 8-step process and risk assessment procedures; sample changes at initiation and approval stages | When: Continuous monitoring via MoC tracking tool; formal audit at least quarterly with minimum 10% of initiated MoCs reviewed | Where: MoC tracking system/tool; MoC quality assurance audit checklists; assurance report to MoC PO | Why: Ensures changes do not bypass hazard assessment and risk mitigation steps; verifies competent staff involved; detects process gaps early; prevents uncontrolled changes reaching implementation | How: (1) MoC Focal Point monitors all initiated changes in system for completeness of scope statement; (2) Quarterly, PO selects representative sample of MoCs (risk-stratified: all High-risk, sample Medium, limited Low); (3) Review against MoC procedure checklist covering Steps 1-8, hazard screening, risk assessment quality, approvals; (4) Document findings in audit report; (5) Report to Asset Management and escalate non-compliance | Evidence: MoC quality assurance audit checklist, signed audit report with findings and recommendations, evidence of management response to findings, tracking of corrective actions
keyActivities: Define MoC quality assurance criteria based on SEAM Standards 8-step process
Develop audit checklist covering process steps, hazard screening, risk assessment, competence, approvals
Establish sampling plan (e.g., 100% High-risk, 30% Medium, 10% Low)
Conduct quarterly MoC audits by MoC Focal Point or independent reviewer
Document findings: scope completeness, hazard identification quality, risk assessment rigor, approval authority
Identify root causes of non-compliance (training gap, resource constraint, procedure clarity)
Report findings to MoC Process Owner and Asset Manager
Implement corrective actions (procedure update, training, coaching)
Track closure of audit findings and effectiveness of corrections
riskAddressed: Process Safety risk: changes proceed without adequate hazard screening or risk assessment; incompetent personnel involved; risk mitigation actions not implemented; scope changes approved without re-review
testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) documented MoC quality assurance plan and checklist exist; (2) MoC audit is performed at least quarterly with documented sample selection and scope; (3) audit checklist covers all 8 MoC steps and SEAM Standards requirements; (4) audit findings documented with evidence (e.g., missing hazard screening, unsigned approvals); (5) root cause analysis performed for non-compliant MoCs; (6) corrective action plan issued and tracked; (7) management review minutes show MoC assurance results discussed; (8) sample recent MoCs checked to verify compliance with identified corrective actions

subProcess: Control of Work Procedure Development and Maintenance
controlName: Control of Work Procedure Documentation and Version Control
controlStatement: Who: Asset HSSE Lead and Process Owner for Permitted/Non-Permitted Work | What: Establish and maintain formal Control of Work procedures covering both Permitted and Non-Permitted Work; define hazards, controls, Step 7 Conversation requirements, SWC verification, and supervision expectations | When: Documented procedures in place before work execution; reviewed and updated minimum annually or upon organizational/operational change; version control enforced | Where: Local Management System repository; Permit Vision (for Permitted Work procedures); documented in asset CoW procedure manual | Why: Provides clear operational guidance ensuring consistent execution of work controls; establishes expectations for Step 7 Conversation, SWC verification, and supervision; reduces risk of hazards not being identified or mitigated at point of work | How: (1) Develop comprehensive CoW procedure addressing high-risk activities in scope (confined space, hot work, isolation, excavation, etc.); (2) Define roles: work performer, permit holder, supervisor, SWC verifier; (3) Specify Step 7 Conversation and SWC verification requirements; (4) Include hazard identification criteria and control hierarchy; (5) Document in LMS with version control, approval, and review schedule; (6) Communicate changes to all affected staff with training | Evidence: Dated and signed CoW procedure with Asset HSSE approval, version history, training records showing staff competence, procedure accessibility in workplace (electronic and hard copy where needed)
keyActivities: Conduct hazard assessment of all work activities in scope (permitted and non-permitted)
Identify high-risk activities requiring Start Work Checks (SWC) and Step 7 Conversation
Draft CoW procedure addressing: work identification, hazard assessment, control selection, supervision, SWC verification
Define roles and competence requirements: work performer, permit holder, supervisor, SWC verifier
Specify Step 7 Conversation requirements: when, who leads, what is discussed
Include escalation process for when work is unsafe to proceed or conditions change
Document procedure in LMS with clear formatting, intent statement, and life-critical steps highlighted
Obtain approval from Asset Manager/HSSE Lead and document date
Implement training program for all staff involved in work execution
Maintain version control and communicate updates via Management of Change
riskAddressed: Without clear procedures, hazards are not consistently identified, controls are not verified at point of risk, supervision is inconsistent, incidents occur due to lack of clarity on safe work practices
testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) formal CoW procedure document exists in LMS with dated approval; (2) procedure covers all in-scope high-risk work activities per SEAM Standards and asset operations; (3) procedure includes hazard identification, control hierarchy, role definitions, Step 7 Conversation, SWC verification; (4) procedure specifies supervision span of control and competence expectations; (5) version control evident (version number, date, approval history); (6) procedure is accessible to work teams (electronic and hard copy); (7) staff can articulate key elements of CoW procedure and their role; (8) training records show staff competence on procedure before work authorization

subProcess: Step 7 Conversation and Start Work Check Execution
controlName: Structured Step 7 Conversation and SWC Verification Before Work Execution
controlStatement: Who: Work supervisor or lead (for Step 7 Conversation); competent SWC Verifier from work party | What: Conduct structured Step 7 Conversation covering: what can go wrong, what barriers/controls are in place, what if barriers fail, what has changed; verify all Start Work Check items completed and physical controls in place before work begins | When: Mandatory for all RAM Severity 4+ work and high-risk activities; conducted immediately before work starts (at point of risk) | Where: At job site/location where work will occur (not office-based); documented in permit record or work log | Why: Ensures all personnel understand hazards and control measures, catches last-minute changes or missing controls, creates psychological safety culture where people speak up about concerns | How: (1) Assemble work party at job location; (2) Conduct Step 7 Conversation using conversation card: what can go wrong, what controls in place, what if controls fail, what has changed; (3) All team members contribute and acknowledge understanding; (4) SWC Verifier independently checks SWC checklist against actual site conditions; (5) Verifier confirms physical controls visually present; (6) If any control missing or concern raised, work pauses and escalated to supervisor; (7) Once all checks green, supervisor/permit holder authorizes work to start | Evidence: Completed Step 7 Conversation record (permit record or work log entry), signed SWC checklist with verifier identity and date, supervisor authorization to proceed, documented any barriers or concerns raised and how addressed
keyActivities: Identify work activities requiring Step 7 Conversation (all RAM Severity 4+)
Schedule Step 7 Conversation for timing immediately before work starts
Assemble work party at job location with permit holder, supervisor, workers, and SWC verifier
Lead Step 7 Conversation: discuss what can go wrong, barriers in place, what if barriers fail, what has changed
Encourage all team members to participate; create psychologically safe environment for raising concerns
Document conversation participants and key discussion points
SWC Verifier independently reviews applicable SWC checklist
Verifier walks through site and visually confirms all physical controls are in place
Verifier makes Go/No-Go decision and documents on SWC checklist
If No-Go, work pauses; concern communicated to supervisor and escalated
Once Go decision confirmed, permit holder or supervisor authorizes work to proceed
Retain records per asset records management policy
riskAddressed: Workers may not understand hazards or how to execute safely; last-minute changes to site conditions not detected; psychological safety lacking (workers do not speak up about concerns); barriers not actually in place despite paperwork; incidents due to gaps between planning and field execution
testingApproach: Auditor observes or reviews recent work execution: (1) Step 7 Conversation completed with all required participants at point of risk (not off-site); (2) conversation card or work log documents discussion of: what can go wrong, what controls in place, what if barriers fail, what has changed; (3) all team members present and engaged in conversation; (4) SWC checklist completed independently by designated verifier; (5) verifier conducts physical verification at site (not from office); (6) all control items on SWC checked as in-place; (7) if concerns raised, documented how escalated and resolved; (8) supervisor/permit holder authorization recorded before work starts; (9) records retained per policy; (10) staff can articulate purpose of Step 7 Conversation and their role in ensuring work is safe to start

controlName: Knowledge Management Focal Point Appointment and Trusted Content Governance
controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager or SEAM Leadership Lead | What: Appoints dedicated Knowledge Management (KM) Focal Point and establishes KM governance framework; defines accountability for organizing and validating trusted content in Single Point of Access (SPoA) and community forums | When: Upon SEAM Standards implementation; formalized in org structure; governance reviewed annually | Where: Org structure documentation, KM procedure, appointment memo, SPoA governance records | Why: Ensures systematic organization of critical knowledge, prevents information fragmentation, enables rapid access to trusted expertise and lessons learned, builds organizational learning capability and reduces repetition of mistakes | How: (1) Identify and appoint KM Focal Point with knowledge management experience; (2) Define responsibilities: SPoA maintenance, content moderation, community activation, LFE integration; (3) Issue appointment memo with authority and accountability; (4) Establish governance framework for content curation and validation by subject matter experts; (5) Document in SPoA Content Management Guide; (6) Provide training on KM tools and platforms (SIGN forums, SPoA, LFE, Expertise Finder); (7) Establish cadence for content review and expert validation | Evidence: Signed appointment memo with date, written KM governance procedure, SPoA access logs showing regular moderation, documented expert validation of critical content, training records
keyActivities: Identify candidate for KM Focal Point role with experience in knowledge organization and community engagement
Draft KM governance framework defining roles (KM FP, SME validators, community leaders)
Issue formal appointment memo signed by Asset Manager or SEAM Leadership
Define accountability for SPoA maintenance, content moderation, and expert validation
Establish process for identifying and tagging expertise areas (processes, risk areas, disciplines)
Create content management guide for categorizing and storing trusted knowledge
Set up moderation process for SIGN forums to ensure quality answers and peer review
Link LFE lessons learned to relevant expertise areas for easy discovery
Maintain Expertise Finder Master List (EFML) with current role and appointment changes
Conduct quarterly review of KM metrics (community participation, content usage, learning adoption)
Provide ongoing training and support to community leaders on KM platforms
riskAddressed: Knowledge becomes siloed and inaccessible; duplicate problem-solving wastes resources; lessons learned are forgotten and mistakes repeated; external expertise goes untapped; organizational learning capacity is weak; SEAM Standards implementation slows without accessible guidance and best practices
testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) KM Focal Point appointed with signed memo and documented authority; (2) KM governance framework and content management guide in place; (3) SPoA is actively maintained with current content moderated by named reviewers; (4) expert subject matter experts identified and appointed in EFML; (5) SIGN forums monitored with documented answers/discussions; (6) LFE lessons learned tagged and categorized per governance framework; (7) community network meetings held with documented attendance and KM discussion topics; (8) staff awareness of where to find expertise (SPoA, SIGN, Expertise Finder); (9) metrics show community participation and content reuse; (10) KM tools accessible and documented (SPoA, LFE links, SIGN forum links available to staff)

controlName: In-Depth Incident Investigation Decision and Investigation Capability
controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager or Operations Lead | What: Establish decision criteria and authority for determining which incidents require in-depth causal learning investigation; maintain competent investigation team (Community of Practice) capable of performing causal-reasoned investigations | When: Documented decision criteria established at asset commissioning; investigation decision made within 72 hours of incident reporting; competence assessments conducted annually | Where: Asset procedure for investigation decision criteria, Investigation Community of Practice roster, investigation sponsor appointment documentation, causal learning training records | Why: Ensures high-learning-value incidents receive appropriate investigation depth; prevents under-investigation (repeating mistakes) and over-investigation (resource dilution); builds organizational capability to identify and address root causes systematically | How: (1) Establish written decision criteria for in-depth investigation (Significant Incidents always; high-potential incidents assessed for learning value; recurrence history; risk profile); (2) Document in investigation decision procedure with clear authority and escalation path; (3) Identify and document competent investigation team members; (4) Ensure team has completed Causal Learning Fundamentals training and maintains competence; (5) Appoint investigation sponsor (senior manager with authority); (6) When significant incident or high-potential incident occurs, convene decision team within 24-48 hours; (7) Assess learning value using documented criteria; (8) Approve investigation scope and resource allocation | Evidence: Written investigation decision criteria and procedure, signed by Asset Manager; roster of competent investigators with training dates; investigation sponsor appointments for initiated investigations; decision meeting minutes showing criteria applied; causal learning training completion records; investigation scoping documents
keyActivities: Develop clear decision criteria for in-depth investigation (SI always; HPI assessed; recurrence; risk assessment)
Document decision process and authority in asset procedure
Identify pool of potential investigators and assess causal learning competence
Arrange Causal Learning Fundamentals training for investigation team members
Appoint investigation sponsor (Operations Lead or senior manager) with authority and authority
Establish investigation decision team composition and meeting cadence
Create incident reporting and notification process to trigger decision team assessment
Develop investigation scoping template defining ToR, team composition, timeline
Allocate minimum 50% FTE capacity for investigation team during active investigations
Conduct annual competence assessment of investigation team members
Document lessons learned from each investigation and update KM platforms (LFE)
riskAddressed: High-potential incidents and Significant Incidents investigated superficially, root causes not identified, similar incidents repeat; organizational learning capacity weak; resources wasted on low-value investigations; knowledge loss when experienced investigators leave; failure to identify systemic causes allows drift toward major incident
testingApproach: Auditor verifies: (1) documented incident investigation decision criteria and procedure in place with Asset Manager approval; (2) criteria clearly distinguish Significant Incidents (always investigate), High Potential (assess learning value), routine incidents (minimal investigation); (3) recent incident records show decision team convened within 72 hours with documented assessment against criteria; (4) documented investigation sponsor appointment for each in-depth investigation; (5) investigation team members have completed Causal Learning Fundamentals training (check training records); (6) investigation community of practice roster maintained with names, competence levels, availability; (7) investigation scoping documents (ToR) are complete and signed; (8) investigation timeline and resource allocation documented; (9) examples of completed investigations with documented root cause analysis and causal threads; (10) high-value learnings documented in LFE with cross-references to investigation findings
